title: What is the oldest American object ever launched into space?
gist: A survey of the oldest pieces of American historical memorabilia flown into orbit, concluding that a copper spike from the USS Constitution's hull (dating to 1797) flown aboard Atlantis on the first shuttle-Mir docking mission in 1995 is the likely oldest genuine Americana artifact launched into space.

themes.theme: Oldest verified Americana artifact in space

insights.claim: A copper spike, an original component of the USS Constitution's hull dating to 1797 and removed from the ship in 1992, is believed to be the oldest genuine piece of Americana ever flown into space, having been carried aboard shuttle Atlantis on the STS-71 mission of June 27-July 7, 1995.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: The 'oldest Americana in space' record is an original artifact rather than a replica, a distinction that drives collectible provenance and museum-loan value.
insights.quote: It was another spike, however, that is believed to be the oldest Americana artifact to have flown into space.

insights.claim: The USS Constitution copper spike flew on STS-71, the first-ever rendezvous and docking between a NASA space shuttle and the former Russian space station Mir, tying the artifact to a milestone in US-Russia spaceflight cooperation.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Context for when US-Russia orbital cooperation began, a relationship whose reversal now shapes NASA and commercial-station strategy.
insights.locator: The spike, which was removed from the ship in 1992

insights.claim: A second USS Constitution artifact, a fragment of wood of similar age on loan from the US Naval Academy Museum, was launched into orbit alongside the Hubble Space Telescope on mission STS-31.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Multiple original Constitution relics have flown, expanding the universe of space-flown historical collectibles that command premiums.
insights.locator: Another Constitution piece, a fragment of wood

themes.theme: Founding-era memorabilia flown by John Glenn

insights.claim: John Glenn, at age 77, became the oldest person to fly into space aboard the 1998 Discovery mission STS-95, decades after his original Mercury flights.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Benchmark data point on human age limits in spaceflight, relevant as commercial operators court older paying passengers.
insights.locator: John Glenn, one of the original Mercury astronauts

insights.claim: Glenn carried a 1993 reprint of Thomas Jefferson's 1801 'Manual of Parliamentary Practice' and a replica of George Washington's 1777 Headquarters Flag on STS-95, but both were reproductions because the originals were deemed too fragile and irreplaceable to launch.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Illustrates why most 'historic' space-flown items are replicas, a key authenticity caveat for anyone valuing space memorabilia.
insights.quote: Both the flag and the manual, though, were reproductions.
insights.locator: Both the flag and the manual

insights.claim: Two 15-inch copper Statue of Liberty statues, cast from copper removed during the statue's ongoing restoration and dating to its 1875-1884 construction, flew aboard Discovery's STS-51D mission in April 1985; one was later melted into copper seals sold to the public by the Statue of Liberty Ellis Island Centennial Commission.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Example of commemorative space-flown objects being commercialized into sellable collectibles, a monetization template.
insights.locator: Did you know that the space shuttle once launched the Statue of Liberty

insights.claim: A 15-star flag flown over Fort McHenry for Flag Day during the bicentennial of the Battle of Baltimore was aboard the International Space Station from November 2014 through June 2015 with NASA astronaut Terry Virts, linking the mission to the original flag that inspired the Star-Spangled Banner in 1814.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Shows the ISS being used as a platform for national commemorative payloads, a soft-power and public-engagement use case.
insights.locator: Another replica flag steeped in American history

insights.claim: A replica of the 1869 17.6-karat gold Golden Spike that completed the First Transcontinental Railroad at Promontory Summit was launched on Atlantis with the STS-38 crew, and that historic site sits near where Northrop Grumman builds and tests solid rocket motors for space launch vehicles.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Rare investor-relevant hook: Northrop Grumman's Utah solid-rocket-motor production remains a supply-chain node for launch and defense programs.
insights.quote: Today, the site is a National Historical Park located near where Northrop Grumman builds and tests solid rocket motors for space launch vehicles.

insights.claim: Objects predating the Revolutionary War have reached orbit, including a 1611 Jamestown 'luggage tag' from the first permanent English settlement flown on Atlantis in 2007, but the article scopes 'Americana' to items dating to 1776 or later.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Clarifies category boundaries that determine which artifacts count as record-holders in the space-memorabilia niche.
